items.headline: Is Stem Cell Therapy for Diabetes in Mexico the Best Choice for Canadians?
items.preview: Stem cell therapy in Mexico offers a promising alternative for Canadians with diabetes.
items.article:
Stem cell therapy in Mexico has become a compelling alternative for Canadians battling Type 1 and Type 2 diabetes. This innovative approach focuses on regenerating pancreatic function and improving insulin sensitivity, offering a new path for managing the disease.
Why this matters: Many patients experience reduced insulin requirements and enhanced energy levels, showcasing its potential benefits. Mexico's advanced regulatory framework allows for the safe administration of high-potency Mesenchymal Stem Cells (MSCs), which are often unavailable in Canada due to regulatory delays.
The therapy works to halt autoimmune attacks on the pancreas for Type 1 diabetes and improves insulin sensitivity while reducing systemic inflammation for Type 2. Patients typically save 50%-70% compared to similar treatments in the US or Panama, making advanced care more accessible.

items.headline: ADC Therapeutics cuts 17% of staff after safety setback
items.preview: ADC Therapeutics announces workforce reduction following a difficult Phase III trial.
items.article:
ADC Therapeutics will cut about 17% of its global workforce as it tries to preserve cash and keep Zynlonta’s regulatory strategy on track after a difficult Phase III readout. The reorganization is expected to generate about US$10 million in annualized savings.
Why it matters: The cuts come after the Phase III LOTIS-5 trial produced mixed results for Zynlonta, which is already approved in the US and Europe for certain types of lymphoma. The company is preparing for a pre-supplemental Biologics License Application meeting with the FDA to discuss a potential regulatory path for Zynlonta.
Investor pressure has led to a significant drop in ADC Therapeutics’ stock since the announcement of the Phase III results, highlighting the challenges faced by the company.

items.headline: BIO 2026: ‘I am alive today because of biotech’
items.preview: Patients share transformative experiences with pioneering therapies at BIO 2026.
items.article:
At the 2026 BIO International Convention, patients reflected on their journeys with groundbreaking therapies for conditions like sickle cell disease and multiple sclerosis. Jennelle Stephenson, who underwent gene therapy for sickle cell disease, shared her story of living pain-free after years of suffering.
Why this matters: These narratives highlight the human impact of biotech advancements, emphasizing the importance of continued innovation in the field. Marci McCue, the first patient to receive CAR-T therapy for MS, and Tom Whitehead, whose daughter was the first pediatric CAR-T patient for leukemia, also shared their experiences, underscoring the hope that biotech brings to patients and families.
The stories serve as a reminder of the potential for biotech to transform lives and the need for ongoing support for research and development.

items.headline: “It’s time to Act”: EU biotech to undergo major changes
items.preview: The EU plans significant reforms to boost its biotech sector competitiveness.
items.article:
The European Union is set to implement the EU Biotech Act to address declining competitiveness in the biotech sector. The Act aims to mobilize €10 billion to support startups and improve access to capital, which is currently lacking in Europe.
Why this matters: The EU's share of global venture capital investment in health biotech is only 7%, and the region has seen a drop in its share of global clinical studies. The proposed reforms aim to streamline regulatory processes and enhance funding opportunities, ensuring that Europe remains a leader in biotech innovation.
The Act also seeks to expedite clinical trials and improve biomanufacturing capabilities, addressing the structural barriers that have hindered the growth of the biotech sector in Europe.

items.headline: Unusual decay: Recombination loss leads to splicing errors in green algae
items.preview: New research reveals splicing errors in green algae due to recombination loss.
items.article:
A study published in PLOS Biology explores the effects of recombination loss in green algae, revealing an increase in aberrant splicing in non-recombining regions. The research highlights how these regions accumulate non-functional transcripts, leading to genomic decay.
Why this matters: Understanding the mechanisms behind splicing errors in algae can provide insights into broader evolutionary processes and the consequences of recombination suppression. The findings suggest that the unique chromatin state of mating-type regions in green algae may contribute to these splicing deficiencies, offering a new perspective on genomic evolution.
Future research is needed to determine the implications of these findings for other non-recombining genomic regions across different species.
